title: Purdue Model Reference for Sector Analysis
verificationNote: Purdue model levels aligned with ISA-95/ISA-99 and IEC 62443. Used for sector-specific zone assignment and security level gap assessment. Device lists and protocols are indicative, not exhaustive.
description: Interactive Purdue model reference mapping each level to typical devices, security requirements, IEC 62443 zones, default security levels, and allowed protocols. Used in conjunction with sector requirements for zone-based security assessment.

levels.name: Process
levels.subtitle: Physical Process & Instrumentation
levels.description: The physical process and the sensors/actuators that interface with it. This is the cyber-physical boundary where digital signals control real-world processes.
securityRequirements: Physical access control to field devices and instrument cabinets
Tamper detection on safety-critical sensors
Redundant measurement channels for critical process variables
Independent high-high / low-low safety interlocks (hardwired)
Calibration verification and tamper-evident seals
HART communication security awareness (no native authentication)
levels.isoZone: IEC 62443 Zone: Process Control Zone (lowest level)
levels.defaultSL: SL applied at Level 1 boundary; physical security controls at Level 0

levels.name: Basic Control
levels.subtitle: Controllers & Safety Systems
levels.description: Controllers that execute real-time control logic — PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers, and Safety Instrumented System controllers. The primary target for process manipulation attacks.
typicalDevices: DCS Controllers (Honeywell C300, ABB AC800M, Emerson DeltaV)
Safety Instrumented System controllers (Triconex, Safety Manager)
securityRequirements: Network segmentation from Level 2 (firewall or managed switch ACLs)
Change management for all PLC/DCS program modifications
Firmware integrity verification before deployment
Physical access control to controller cabinets and panels
SIS controllers on independent network from basic process control
PLC/RTU authentication where supported by device firmware
Backup of all controller programs with version control
Restriction of engineering protocol access to authorised workstations only
levels.isoZone: IEC 62443 Zone: Basic Process Control System (BPCS) Zone / SIS Zone (separate)

levels.name: Supervisory
levels.subtitle: HMI, SCADA & Engineering
levels.description: Human-machine interfaces, SCADA servers, and engineering workstations that provide operator visibility and control. Windows/Linux-based systems with standard OS vulnerabilities.
securityRequirements: Application whitelisting on HMI and operator workstations
OT-vendor-approved patch management
No internet access on Level 2 workstations
Jump server for engineering workstation remote access
Multi-factor authentication for engineering tools
USB port control and removable media restrictions
Anti-malware with OT-compatible configuration
Screen lock and session timeout policies
Backup and recovery procedures for SCADA configurations

levels.name: Operations
levels.subtitle: Historian, MES & Site Operations
levels.description: Site-wide operational systems — historians, MES, batch management, and advanced process control. Aggregates data from multiple Level 2 systems and interfaces with business systems via the IDMZ.
typicalDevices: Process historian (AVEVA PI, Canary Labs, Ignition)
Active Directory domain controller (OT domain, if used)
securityRequirements: Separate network segment from Level 2 and IDMZ
Historian with strict access controls and query restrictions
MES input validation on all data from Level 2
Database hardening (disable unnecessary stored procedures, default accounts)
No direct connectivity to internet or corporate email
OT domain isolation from IT domain (if AD used in OT)
Backup and disaster recovery for all Level 3 systems
Change management for MES and historian configuration changes

levels.name: DMZ
levels.subtitle: Industrial Demilitarized Zone
levels.description: The critical security boundary between OT (Levels 0-3) and IT (Levels 4-5). All data exchange between OT and IT must pass through the IDMZ. No direct traffic between OT and IT networks is permitted.
securityRequirements: Deny-by-default firewall rules at BOTH IDMZ boundaries
No direct network path between IT and OT — broken by IDMZ services
Data diode for OT-to-IT data export where feasible
Jump server with MFA, session recording, and time-limited access
All IDMZ systems hardened and included in patch management scope
Regular review of all permitted traffic flows through IDMZ
File transfer through IDMZ must include malware scanning
IDMZ monitoring for anomalous traffic patterns

levels.name: Enterprise
levels.subtitle: ERP, Email & Business Systems
levels.description: Business information systems — ERP, supply chain management, business analytics, corporate email. Standard IT security applies. Must never have direct connectivity to OT Levels 0-3.
securityRequirements: IDMZ enforces separation from OT Level 3
No direct network connectivity to OT levels 0-3
Standard IT security controls (ISO 27001 / NIST CSF scope)
Phishing protection and email security
Endpoint detection and response (EDR)
Identity and access management
Security awareness training for all users
levels.isoZone: Outside IEC 62443 scope — ISO 27001 / NIST CSF applies

levels.name: Internet/Cloud
levels.subtitle: External Networks & Cloud Services
levels.description: Internet connectivity, cloud services, and external partner networks. Must have no direct connectivity to OT environment. Cloud-based OT services (historian, analytics, remote monitoring) must route through IDMZ.
typicalDevices: Cloud-based OT platforms (AVEVA Connect, Honeywell Forge)
securityRequirements: No direct connectivity from internet to OT environment
All OT remote access through IDMZ jump server — never direct VPN to OT
Cloud OT platforms connected via IDMZ gateway only
IIoT cloud connectivity through industrial IoT gateway in IDMZ
DDoS protection for internet-facing services
Third-party risk assessment for cloud OT service providers
levels.isoZone: Outside IEC 62443 scope — perimeter security applies
levels.defaultSL: N/A — no OT assets at this level

dataFlowRules.condition: Deny-by-default; broken by IDMZ services (no direct pass-through)
Firewall with OT-aware rules; OPC/Modbus permitted
Engineering protocols permitted from authorised workstations only
PROHIBITED — must traverse IDMZ. This is the cardinal rule of OT security architecture.
PROHIBITED — no direct internet-to-OT connectivity under any circumstances.
OT-to-IT data export via IDMZ (data diode preferred for critical environments)
